title: 11_orders_per_month.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Orders per Month

You're plotting how order volume changes month to month. The plan: pull the month out of each `order_date` and count orders per month. But `order_date.dt.month` throws an `AttributeError` about the `.dt` accessor.

The `order_date dtype: object` line explains it â€” the dates loaded as plain **text**, not as real dates, so the calendar tools don't apply yet. Convert the column to real datetimes first, then the monthly breakdown works.
initialCode:
import pandas as pd

orders = pd.read_csv("orders.csv")
print("order_date dtype:", orders["order_date"].dtype)

# Orders per month
orders["month"] = orders["order_date"].dt.month
print(orders.groupby("month").size())
traceback:
order_date dtype: object
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 7
      6 # Orders per month
----> 7 orders["month"] = orders["order_date"].dt.month

AttributeError: Can only use .dt accessor with datetimelike values
correctOutput:
order_date dtype: datetime64[ns]
month
1    4
2    3
3    5
dtype: int64

options.label: Convert the column with pd.to_datetime, then use .dt.month
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd

orders = pd.read_csv("orders.csv")
orders["order_date"] = pd.to_datetime(orders["order_date"])
print("order_date dtype:", orders["order_date"].dtype)

# Orders per month
orders["month"] = orders["order_date"].dt.month
print(orders.groupby("month").size())
options.resultLog:
order_date dtype: datetime64[ns]
month
1    4
2    3
3    5
dtype: int64
options.rationale: `read_csv` left the dates as text (`object`), and the `.dt` accessor only works on real datetime values. `pd.to_datetime` parses the strings into a true `datetime64` column, after which `.dt.month` (and `.dt.day`, `.dt.year`, `.dt.weekday`, â€¦) all work, giving an integer month you can group by.

options.label: Convert the column but read .month directly off the Series
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd

orders = pd.read_csv("orders.csv")
print("order_date dtype:", orders["order_date"].dtype)

# Orders per month
orders["month"] = pd.to_datetime(orders["order_date"]).month
print(orders.groupby("month").size())
options.resultLog:
order_date dtype: object
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 7
----> 7 orders["month"] = pd.to_datetime(orders["order_date"]).month

AttributeError: 'Series' object has no attribute 'month'
options.rationale: The conversion is right, but `.month` lives on individual datetime *values*, not on a whole Series. On a Series you reach the calendar parts through the `.dt` accessor: `pd.to_datetime(orders["order_date"]).dt.month`. Forgetting `.dt` is the most common slip right after converting.

options.label: Slice the month out of the date text
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd

orders = pd.read_csv("orders.csv")
print("order_date dtype:", orders["order_date"].dtype)

# Orders per month
orders["month"] = orders["order_date"].str.split("-").str[1]
print(orders.groupby("month").size())
options.resultLog:
order_date dtype: object
month
01    4
02    3
03    5
dtype: int64
options.rationale: Splitting the string does pull out a month-like piece, but it's brittle and wrong-typed: the month is text (`'01'`, `'02'`) not a number, it assumes every date is exactly `YYYY-MM-DD`, and it breaks on any other format or a real date object. Convert with `pd.to_datetime` so `.dt.month` gives a real integer month that works regardless of the source format.

hints: Read the printed dtype: `object` means the dates are still text. The `.dt` accessor â€” your gateway to month, day, weekday â€” only works once the column is a real datetime.
`pd.to_datetime(orders["order_date"])` parses the text into real dates. Assign it back, then use `.dt.month` (note the `.dt`).
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`read_csv` stores anything it isn't sure is numeric as text, so `order_date` arrives with dtype `object` â€” strings like `"2024-03-15"`. pandas exposes date parts (month, day, weekday) through the `.dt` accessor, but `.dt` is only valid on a real `datetime64` column. Calling `.dt.month` on text raises `AttributeError: Can only use .dt accessor with datetimelike values`.

### Why the fix is correct
`pd.to_datetime(orders["order_date"])` parses the strings into genuine `datetime64` values; assigning it back changes the column's dtype. Now `.dt.month` returns an integer month for each row, and you can group and count. Converting once at load time is far more robust than slicing the text by hand: it handles different formats, gives you the whole `.dt` toolkit (`.dt.year`, `.dt.day_name()`, `.dt.to_period("M")`), and keeps the values typed as real dates for sorting and arithmetic.
recruiterReview: Good diagnosis â€” you read the dtype, recognized the dates were text, and converted them properly instead of hacking the month out of the string. Reaching for `pd.to_datetime` unlocks the entire `.dt` toolkit and keeps the data sortable and arithmetic-friendly. Two pro tips: pass `format=` when you know the layout (it's faster and stricter), and consider `.dt.to_period("M")` when you want year+month together so January 2023 and January 2024 don't collapse into the same bucket. Approved. âś…

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Working with Dates That Loaded as Text

Time-based analysis â€” trends by month, day-of-week patterns, gaps between events â€” all depends on your dates being *real* dates. Most of the time they don't start that way.

### The problem
`pd.read_csv` doesn't parse dates by default; it leaves them as text (dtype `object`). Text can't do date math. The clue is always the dtype:
```python
orders["order_date"].dtype   # object  â†’ it's text
```

### The .dt accessor
pandas keeps date operations behind a special accessor called `.dt`, available only on real `datetime64` columns:
```python
orders["order_date"].dt.year
orders["order_date"].dt.month
orders["order_date"].dt.day_name()   # 'Monday', ...
```
If the column is still text, `.dt` raises `AttributeError`.

### The fix: pd.to_datetime
```python
orders["order_date"] = pd.to_datetime(orders["order_date"])
orders["order_date"].dtype   # datetime64[ns]
```
Now every `.dt` operation works.

### Series vs single value
A subtle trap: `.month` exists on a single `Timestamp`, but on a **Series** you must go through `.dt`:
```python
ts = pd.to_datetime("2024-03-15"); ts.month          # 3   (single value)
orders["order_date"].dt.month                          # Series of months
```

### A tiny worked example
```python
s = pd.Series(["2024-01-10", "2024-02-02"])
s = pd.to_datetime(s)
s.dt.month.tolist()   # [1, 2]
```

### Don't slice the string
`order_date.str[5:7]` or `.str.split("-")` looks tempting, but it gives text months, assumes one exact format, and breaks on real date objects. Convert once and use `.dt`.

### How to spot it
`AttributeError: Can only use .dt accessor with datetimelike values` means the column is still text â€” convert it.

### Common beginner mistakes
- Forgetting that `read_csv` doesn't auto-parse dates.
- Using `.month` on a Series instead of `.dt.month`.
- Slicing the date string by hand (brittle, text output).
- Grouping by month alone across multiple years, merging different years together (use `.dt.to_period("M")`).

You'll hit this the first time you build any trend over time.

conceptCard:
This mission is about **dates that loaded as text**.

- `read_csv` leaves dates as text (dtype `object`) by default.
- The `.dt` accessor (for month, day, weekday) only works on real datetime values.
- Convert with `pd.to_datetime(...)` first.

The code asks for the month before converting, and the `.dt` accessor fails. Convert first.

lineNotes.noteMd: Prints the column's dtype. `object` means the dates are still **text**, so date tools won't work yet.
Uses `.dt.month`, but `.dt` only works on real datetimes â€” and this column is text. Convert it with `pd.to_datetime(...)` first.

takeaway: Convert text dates with pd.to_datetime() before using the .dt accessor for month/day/etc.
